docs.title: Dapil 🏎️💨
docs.text:
Ultra-high-performance Python web framework powered by Rust and Axum.

Dapil is a next-generation Python web framework designed for extreme performance. It bridges the gap between Python's developer productivity and Rust's world-class efficiency.

By leveraging a specialized Single Actor GIL Model, Dapil achieves throughput that redefines what is possible for Python web services.

docs.title: Key Highlights
docs.text:
• 8x Faster than FastAPI: Built on the world-class Axum/Tokio stack.
  • Zero GIL Contention: Isolated worker thread ensures the Python interpreter runs at 100% efficiency.
  • Automatic Observability: Seamless Rust-to-Python logging bridge.
  • Modern Simplicity: Developer experience inspired by FastAPI.

docs.title: Usage Example
docs.text:
import dapil

app = dapil.App()

@app.get("/")
def hello():
    return "Hello from Dapil!"

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.serve()

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
Framework Requests/Sec Relative Performance Dapil (Extreme) ~29,660 8.3x vs FastAPI Django-Bolt (Rust) ~19,510 5.5x FastAPI ~3,500 1.0x

[!IMPORTANT] Dapil is currently under active development. While performance is extreme, the feature set is still expanding.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: Dapil is designed with a "Rust-First, Python-Friendly" philosophy. It leverages the robust Axum web framework for networking while providing a clean, Pythonic interface for business logic.

docs.title: The Core Problem: GIL Contention
docs.text: In traditional Python/Rust bridges (like those using spawn_blocking), multiple threads often compete for the Python Global Interpreter Lock (GIL). This contention creates a bottleneck where threads spend more time waiting for the lock than executing code, severely limiting throughput.

docs.title: The Solution: Single Actor GIL Model
docs.text:
Dapil implements a Single Actor pattern for GIL management:

graph TD
    Client[HTTP Client] --> Axum[Axum/Tokio Rust Runtime]
    Axum --> Channel[(High-Performance Flume Channel)]
    Channel --> Worker[Dedicated Python Worker Thread]
    Worker --> GIL[Acquire GIL]
    GIL --> Handler[Execute Python Handler]
    Handler --> Result[Return Result]
    Result --> Channel
    Channel --> Axum
    Axum --> Client

docs.title: 1. Multi-Threaded I/O (Rust)
docs.text: Incoming connections and HTTP parsing are handled by Tokio, Rust's industry-standard asynchronous runtime. This allows Dapil to handle massive concurrency (thousands of simultaneous connections) without blocking.

docs.title: 2. Isolated Python Execution
docs.text: Instead of calling Python handlers directly from the async task (which would require locking/unlocking the GIL constantly), Dapil sends a message to a dedicated worker thread.

docs.title: 3. Worker Thread (The Actor)
docs.text:
The worker thread is the only thread that interacts with the Python interpreter. It: - Waits for a task on a lock-free channel. - Acquires the GIL once. - Processes the task. - Sends the response back.

This "Single Actor" approach ensures that the Python interpreter is always running at peak efficiency, as there is absolutely zero lock contention from other Rust threads.

docs.title: Optimized Memory Management
docs.text:
• String Interning: Frequently used paths and methods are handled efficiently.
  • Allocation Minimization: We use direct byte buffers where possible to avoid unnecessary Python-to-Rust string conversions.
  • Link-Time Optimization (LTO): When built in release mode, the entire binary (Rust + CPython bridge) is optimized as a single unit by the compiler.

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text: Performance is the cornerstone of Dapil. These benchmarks compare Dapil against industry standards and other high-performance frameworks.

docs.title: Hardware & Environment
docs.text:
• CPU: x86_64 Linux
  • Concurrency: 50 concurrent connections
  • Total Requests: 10,000
  • Build Mode: Release (LTO enabled, 1 codegen unit)

docs.title: Throughput Comparison
docs.text: Framework Requests/Sec Latency (Mean) Dapil (Extreme) 29,661 1.6 ms Django-Bolt 19,511 2.5 ms Django (Gunicorn) 5,623 8.8 ms FastAPI (Uvicorn) 3,563 14.0 ms

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text:
1. Vs FastAPI (8.32x): FastAPI (and Uvicorn) are bottlenecked by Python's asynchronous overhead and GIL management when handling concurrent I/O. Dapil offloads all I/O to Rust, leaving Python only responsible for the business logic.
  2. Vs Django-Bolt (1.52x): While Django-Bolt also uses a Rust core, Dapil's specialized Single Actor worker model provides lower overhead for simple dispatching, ensuring the GIL is held optimally.
  3. Vs Standard Django (5.27x): Standard sync Django is limited by worker process/thread overhead. Dapil's hybrid model provides the efficiency of an async event loop with the simplicity of sync handlers.

docs.title: Reproducing Benchmarks
docs.text: We use ab (Apache Benchmark) for reliable throughput measurement.

docs.title: 1. Run the Dapil server
docs.text: python examples/hello_world/main.py

docs.title: 3. Comparison Scripts
docs.text: You can find the scripts used for other frameworks in the benchmarks/ directory: - fastapi_app.py - django_app.py

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Dapil combines the best of both worlds: Rust's performance and Python's productivity.

docs.title: 🚀 Extreme Performance
docs.text: Dapil is built on the Axum networking stack and uses a specialized Single Actor GIL Model to maximize throughput. This allows it to reach nearly 30,000 requests per second on a single worker thread.

docs.title: 🌉 The Rust-Python Bridge
docs.text: We use PyO3 to create a seamless connection between Rust and Python. This bridge is optimized for low-latency dispatching and efficient data conversion.

docs.title: 📊 Automatic Observability
docs.text: Dapil bridges Rust's structured tracing events directly into Python's standard logging module. This means: - No manual logging setup in Rust required. - Logs appear in your Python log handlers automatically. - Performance diagnostics are captured at every level of the stack.

docs.title: 🎨 Clean API Design
docs.text:
Inspired by FastAPI, Dapil uses a simple, decorator-based approach to routing.

  • @app.get("/path")
  • @app.post("/path")
  • Automatic HTTP method handling.

docs.title: 🛠️ Advanced Build System
docs.text: Powered by maturin, Dapil is easy to build and develop. - Release mode optimizations enable fat Link-Time Optimization (LTO). - Single-unit codegen for maximum performance.

docs.title: Logging & Observability
docs.text: Dapil provides high-performance logging out of the box. It bridges the powerful Rust tracing ecosystem with Python's standard logging module.

docs.title: Automatic Initialization
docs.text:
Logging is automatically initialized when you create a dapil.App instance. You don't need to call any setup functions manually.

import dapil
import logging

# Configure Python logging normally
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

app = dapil.App() # Rust-Python logging bridge starts here

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
1. Rust Side: Dapil uses the tracing crate for internal performance and diagnostic events.
  2. The Bridge: We use tracing-log to convert tracing events into standard log records, and pyo3-log to forward those records into Python.
  3. Python Side: Python's logging module receives these events as if they were emitted by a standard Python package.

docs.title: Example Output
docs.text:
When you run a Dapil app, you'll see structured logs like this in your terminal:

INFO:dapil:Dapil serving on http://0.0.0.0:8080
INFO:dapil:Incoming request: GET /
INFO:dapil:Response sent: 200 OK in 1.2ms

docs.title: Manual Setup (Optional)
docs.text:
If you need to initialize logging before creating the App, you can use the setup_logging function:

import dapil
dapil.setup_logging()

This is useful if you want to capture logs from the initialization process itself.
